title: Users, Groups & Authentication
description: Linux is a multi-user operating system from the ground up. Every process runs as a specific user, every file has an owner and group, and access decisions are made by the kernel based on user/group IDs. At Enterprise, understanding user management, PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules), and sudo policies is critical for security, compliance, and least-privilege deployments.

sections.title: User & Group Fundamentals
sections.description: Users are identified by UID (User ID) and groups by GID (Group ID). The mapping is stored in /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow (passwords), /etc/group, and /etc/gshadow. UID 0 is root — the superuser with unrestricted access. At Enterprise, human engineers almost never use root — they sudo to specific commands.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Key files ===
cat /etc/passwd          # User accounts: username:x:UID:GID:comment:home:shell
cat /etc/shadow          # Password hashes (root only)
cat /etc/group           # Group definitions: groupname:x:GID:member1,member2
cat /etc/gshadow         # Group passwords (rarely used)

# Understanding /etc/passwd format:
# kallol:x:1000:1000:Kallol:/home/kallol:/bin/bash
# [1] username
# [2] password placeholder (x = hash in /etc/shadow)
# [3] UID (User ID)
# [4] GID (Primary Group ID)
# [5] GECOS (user info, often empty)
# [6] Home directory
# [7] Login shell

# === Adding users ===
sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash -G sudo,docker kallol
# -m  create home directory
# -s  login shell
# -G  supplementary groups
# -c  comment (full name)

sudo useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /usr/sbin/nologin myapp
# System user (UID < 1000), no login, no home

sudo passwd kallol         # Set/change password
sudo usermod -aG docker kallol   # Add to group (append, don't forget -a!)
sudo userdel -r kallol     # Delete user + home + mail spool

# === Listing users ===
who                       # Currently logged in users
w                         # Detailed who (login time, idle, what running)
users                     # Just usernames
id                        # Current user's UID, GID, groups
id kallol                 # Specific user's IDs
finger kallol             # User info (may need apt install finger)

# === Listing groups ===
groups                    # Current user's groups
groups kallol             # Specific user's groups
getent group sudo         # Members of 'sudo' group
lid -g sudo               # List members (may need apt install libuser)

sections.title: sudo: Delegated Privilege
sections.description: sudo allows authorized users to run commands as another user (typically root). At Enterprise, sudo is the primary mechanism for privileged access — root login is disabled. The sudoers file (/etc/sudoers) is a critical security boundary and must be edited with visudo to prevent syntax errors that could lock you out.
sections.codeBlock:
# === /etc/sudoers basics (edit with visudo!) ===

# Simple rules:
kallol ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL              # kallol can do anything
kallol ALL=(ALL:ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL    # No password prompt (dev VMs only)

# Command-level restrictions:
kallol ALL=(root) /usr/bin/systemctl restart nginx
kallol ALL=(root) /usr/bin/journalctl

# Group-based:
%sudo  ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL              # sudo group members have full access
%admin ALL=(ALL) ALL                  # admin group

# === sudo command usage ===
sudo -l                                # List allowed commands for current user
sudo -u www-data whoami               # Run as www-data
sudo -s                                # Root shell (not recommended)
sudo -i                                # Root login shell (loads root's profile)
sudo -E                                # Preserve environment variables

# === /etc/sudoers.d/ — Drop-in files ===
# Better than editing /etc/sudoers directly:
# /etc/sudoers.d/deploy
%deploy ALL=(root) /usr/local/bin/deploy.sh

# === Security best practices ===
# 1. Defaults: !root_sudo         — root should use sudo too (for audit trail)
# 2. Defaults: log_output         — log all sudo commands
# 3. Defaults: timestamp_timeout=5  — re-authenticate after 5 min
# 4. Cmnd_Alias for command groups

Cmnd_Alias NETWORKING = /sbin/ip, /sbin/ss, /sbin/tcpdump
Cmnd_Alias SERVICES = /usr/bin/systemctl *
kallol ALL=(root) NETWORKING, SERVICES

sections.title: PAM: Pluggable Authentication Modules
sections.description: PAM separates authentication logic from applications. When you SSH in, log in on a TTY, or run sudo, the application uses PAM modules to authenticate. At Enterprise, PAM is configured to enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA), LDAP/SSO integration, and hardware key requirements.
sections.codeBlock:
# === PAM configuration files ===
ls /etc/pam.d/
# common-auth        — Authentication methods
# common-account     — Account restrictions (expiry, time)
# common-password    — Password quality rules
# common-session     — Session setup (environment, limits)

# === PAM module types ===
# auth     — Verify identity (password, MFA, SSH key)
# account  — Check account status (expired, locked, time-based)
# password — Update authentication tokens
# session  — Set up environment (open sessions, mount home)

# === Control flags ===
# requisite  — Fail immediately if this module fails
# required   — Fail after all modules run (don't reveal which failed)
# sufficient — If this passes, skip remaining modules
# optional   — Used only if no other module succeeds

# === Example: /etc/pam.d/common-auth ===
auth    [success=1 default=ignore]      pam_unix.so nullok
auth    requisite                       pam_deny.so
auth    required                        pam_permit.so

# === /etc/security/limits.conf (PAM limits) ===
# These apply to PAM sessions (SSH, login, etc.)
kallol soft nofile 65536
kallol hard nofile 65536
@deploy soft nproc 4096

# === Check PAM config ===
sudo pam-auth-update           # Ubuntu: enable/disable modules
pam_tally2 --user kallol       # Show failed login count
sudo pam_tally2 --user kallol --reset  # Reset failed count

sections.title: Service Accounts & Least Privilege
sections.description: Every daemon should run as its own dedicated user with minimum required permissions. At Enterprise, 'root in containers' is a common security finding — each service must have a dedicated non-root user. The principle of least privilege (PoLP) means: no process should have access rights beyond what it strictly needs.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Creating a service account (best practices) ===
sudo useradd \
    --system \
    --no-create-home \
    --shell /usr/sbin/nologin \
    --user-group \
    myapp

# Verify:
grep myapp /etc/passwd
# myapp:x:998:998::/nonexistent:/usr/sbin/nologin

# === Grant minimum permissions ===
# Give service account access to specific directories
sudo mkdir -p /var/log/myapp /var/lib/myapp /etc/myapp
sudo chown myapp:myapp /var/log/myapp /var/lib/myapp /etc/myapp
sudo chmod 750 /var/log/myapp /var/lib/myapp /etc/myapp

# === Capabilities (fine-grained root privileges) ===
# Instead of running as root, grant specific capabilities:
# CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE  — bind to ports < 1024
# CAP_SYS_TIME          — change system clock
# CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE      — bypass file permission checks

# In systemd unit:
[Service]
User=myapp
AmbientCapabilities=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE
CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE

# === Docker: running as non-root ===
FROM node:22-alpine
RUN addgroup -S myapp && adduser -S myapp -G myapp
USER myapp
COPY --chown=myapp:myapp . /app

# === /etc/login.defs ===
# Global user creation defaults:
# UID_MIN 1000
# UID_MAX 60000
# SYS_UID_MIN 100
# SYS_UID_MAX 999
# PASS_MAX_DAYS 90

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'You SSH into a server and get Permission denied (publickey). What do you check?'

A: 'First, verify my SSH key is in ~/.ssh/authorized_keys on the server with correct permissions (600). Check /etc/ssh/sshd_config: PubkeyAuthentication yes, PasswordAuthentication no (usually). Check ~/.ssh permissions (700) and that the user's home directory is not group-writable. Check /etc/security/access.conf and PAM config in /etc/pam.d/sshd. Also check SELinux/AppArmor status with getenforce. At Enterprise, SSH key rotation is automated, but manual troubleshooting still requires knowing these checks.'

Q: 'What's the difference between su and sudo?'

A: 'su switches to another user completely (requires their password), starting a new shell. sudo runs a single command as another user (requires your password). sudo is preferred because: (1) it creates an audit trail, (2) it grants specific commands, not full access, (3) it doesn't require sharing the root password. At Enterprise, su is effectively banned — all privilege elevation goes through sudo with centralized audit logging.'

Q: 'A user left the company. What steps do you take?'

A: 'Immediately disable their account: sudo usermod -L username (lock password) and remove SSH keys. Revoke sudo access: remove from sudoers. Kill their active sessions: pkill -u username. Archive their home directory for compliance. Remove the account after the compliance retention period. At Enterprise, this is automated in the HR → IT pipeline — when an employee is terminated in Workday, their accounts are deprovisioned within minutes across all systems.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• passwd(5) man page — Format of /etc/passwd
  • sudoers(5) man page — Complete sudoers syntax reference
  • Arch Linux PAM Wiki — Excellent reference on PAM modules and configuration

analogy.title: Users as Club Members
analogy.description: Each user has a membership card (UID). Groups are clubs - members share access to club resources (files). Root is the club owner with master keys. sudo is a temporary VIP pass. The /etc/passwd is the member directory; /etc/shadow holds the secret handshakes.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: User & Group Fundamentals
practiceLabs.task: Run `cat` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man cat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: sudo: Delegated Privilege
practiceLabs.task: Run `sudo` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man sudo` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.task: Run `who` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man who` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `cat` | User & Group Fundamentals |
| `sudo` | User & Group Fundamentals |
| `who` | User & Group Fundamentals |
| `kallol` | sudo: Delegated Privilege |
| `sudo` | sudo: Delegated Privilege |
| `Cmnd_Alias` | sudo: Delegated Privilege |
| `ls` | PAM: Pluggable Authentication Modules |
| `auth` | PAM: Pluggable Authentication Modules |
| `kallol` | PAM: Pluggable Authentication Modules |
| `sudo` | Service Accounts & Least Privilege |
| `myapp` | Service Accounts & Least Privilege |
| `grep` | Service Accounts & Least Privilege |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man cat`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: users operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is User & Group Fundamentals?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The User & Group Fundamentals is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `cat` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the users directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a users directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a users scenario, your monitoring alerts show cat failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  User management is often overlooked by engineers focused on 'the fun stuff' — distributed systems, ML pipelines, Kubernetes
productionTips: production engineer, you will spend significant time on access control
Production Tip:  Never, ever disable password expiry for human accounts
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, users, groups & authentication is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
